message:
feat(wa-core): add chaos testing harness for fault injection (wa-37x.3)

Introduces a test-only FaultInjector framework that can inject failures
at specific runtime points (DB writes/reads, WezTerm CLI, pattern
detection, retention cleanup, config reload, webhook delivery).

Key components:
- FaultPoint enum for injection sites with zero prod overhead
- FaultMode variants: AlwaysFail, FailNTimes, FailWithProbability,
  Delay, DelayThenFail
- ChaosScenario builder with declarative assertions
- Pre-built scenarios: db_write_failure, wezterm_unavailable,
  pattern_engine_failure, db_corruption, cascading_failures
- 20 unit tests covering all modes and scenarios

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-27f3","title":"Add wa robot workflow status command","description":"# wa robot workflow status

## Current State (CLI Stub Done)
✅ CLI subcommand structure added
✅ JSON schema created (wa-robot-workflow-status.json)
❌ Implementation returns not-implemented error

## What Remains
Query workflow_executions table for execution state.

## Implementation
1. Accept execution_id parameter
2. Query workflow_executions + workflow_step_log tables
3. Return status, current_step, step_logs, timestamps, error

## Testing Requirements
- Unit: Returns execution status by ID
- Unit: Shows step progress accurately
- Unit: Error code stability (E_EXECUTION_NOT_FOUND)
- E2E: See bd-qvbz for lifecycle scenario

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Queries workflow_executions table
- [ ] Returns real-time step progress
- [ ] Includes step_logs array
- [ ] JSON validates against schema
- [ ] Unit + E2E tests pass with detailed logging

 {"id":"bd-2kl3","title":"[EPIC] Phase 3: Optimize Remaining Lua (user-var hook) — If Still Needed","description":"## Overview

After Phase 1 (remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA), the only remaining Lua code is the user-var forwarding hook. This epic covers potential optimizations if that hook still causes performance issues.

**Priority P3**: This is a contingency. The user-var hook triggers infrequently (only when agents emit signals), so it's unlikely to be a bottleneck. Only pursue if performance testing shows issues.

## Remaining Lua Code

After Phase 1, wezterm.lua will contain only:

\`\`\`lua
-- Forward user-var events to wa daemon
wezterm.on('user-var-changed', function(window, pane, name, value)
  if name:match('^wa%-') then
    wezterm.background_child_process {
      'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
      '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
      '--name', name,
      '--value', value
    }
  end
end)
\`\`\`

## Why This Is Likely Fine

1. **Low frequency**: user-var-changed only fires when agents emit OSC 1337 sequences
2. **Filtered**: Only wa-prefixed vars trigger the handler
3. **Efficient check**: String prefix match is fast in Lua
4. **Non-blocking**: wezterm.background_child_process doesn't block the UI

## Potential Optimizations (If Needed)

### Option A: Remove Name Prefix Check in Lua

Move filtering to wa CLI:

**Before (filter in Lua)**:
\`\`\`lua
if name:match('^wa%-') then
  -- spawn process
end
\`\`\`

**After (filter in wa)**:
\`\`\`lua
wezterm.background_child_process {
  'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
  '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
  '--name', name,
  '--value', value
}
-- wa CLI ignores non-wa-prefixed vars
\`\`\`

**Trade-off**: More process spawns, but simpler Lua.

### Option B: Batching (Complex)

If many user-vars are emitted rapidly:
- Collect in Lua table
- Emit batch every N ms
- Reduces process spawns

**Trade-off**: Added complexity, latency.

### Option C: Replace with Native (Phase 2)

If using vendored WezTerm (Phase 2), user-var events go through native path anyway. No Lua needed.

## When to Pursue This Epic

1. Complete Phase 1
2. Benchmark: Is WezTerm still slow with just user-var hook?
3. Profile: Is user-var hook actually the bottleneck?
4. If yes to both: implement optimizations

## Success Criteria

- User-var forwarding works with acceptable performance
- No perceptible WezTerm slowdown during agent operation
- Latency for user-var events <100ms

## References

- USERVAR_FORWARDING_LUA: crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs lines 33-47
- OSC 1337 user-vars: https://iterm2.com/documentation-escape-codes.html","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:51:22.219783156Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T21:51:35.242788626Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2kl3","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:51:35.242745746Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2kl3","depends_on_id":"bd-2oby","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:51:22.233097688Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2kl8","title":"Change wa robot workflow to require explicit 'run' subcommand","description":"## Goal
Change `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id>` to `wa robot workflow run <name> <pane_id>` for consistency with planned workflow list/status/abort subcommands.

## Breaking Change
This is a breaking change. Agents using `wa robot workflow` must update to `wa robot workflow run`.

## Implementation
1. Add `WorkflowSubcommand` enum with `Run`, `List`, `Status`, `Abort` variants
2. Change `RobotCommands::Workflow` to take subcommand instead of positional args
3. Update quick-start guide examples
4. Update error messages to reference new syntax

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa robot workflow run <name> <pane_id>` works as before
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id>` shows helpful error pointing to new syntax
- Quick-start guide reflects new command structure","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:21:08.619598006Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-22T18:36:54.068422868Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T18:36:54.068365820Z","close_reason":"Implemented: workflow run verb change with subcommand pattern","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2kl8","depends_on_id":"bd-h1ai","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:58Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2l5s","title":"[EPIC] Phase 1: Remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA — Immediate Lua-Free Win","description":"## Overview

This sub-epic implements **Option 1** from the parent epic: completely remove the STATUS_UPDATE_LUA Lua snippet that hooks into WezTerm's \`update-status\` event. This is the **recommended first step** because it delivers immediate performance gains with zero WezTerm source modifications.

## Why This Works

wa's existing architecture already provides the data we need without Lua:

### 1. Pane Metadata (dimensions, title, domain, cwd)
wa's ingest loop already calls \`wezterm cli list --format json\` periodically. This returns:
\`\`\`json
[
  {"pane_id": 0, "title": "...", "domain": "local", "cwd": "/path", ...}
]
\`\`\`

**No Lua needed** — wa can poll this at any desired interval.

### 2. Alt-Screen Detection
The STATUS_UPDATE_LUA currently reports \`is_alt_screen\`. We can detect this **without Lua** by:
- Parsing escape sequences in the captured terminal output stream
- ESC[?1049h = enter alt-screen (smcup)
- ESC[?1049l = leave alt-screen (rmcup)

wa's pattern engine can easily detect these sequences.

### 3. Cursor Position
Rarely needed for wa's core mission (agent state detection). If ever needed:
- Can be inferred from captured output
- Or requested on-demand via \`wezterm cli get-text\` which includes cursor info

## What We Lose

- **Real-time push notifications** of pane state changes → Replaced by **polling**
- Polling interval determines detection latency (configurable, e.g., 200ms vs 2000ms)

## What We Gain

- **Dramatic WezTerm performance improvement** — no Lua handler on every frame
- **Simpler architecture** — fewer moving parts, no Lua-Rust FFI
- **No external process spawning** every 2 seconds per pane

## Implementation Plan

1. **Add alt-screen detection via escape sequences** (in pattern engine)
2. **Ensure ingest loop provides all needed metadata** (already mostly does)
3. **Remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA from setup.rs**
4. **Remove --from-status CLI handling** (cleanup)
5. **Update tests and E2E scenarios**
6. **Document migration for existing users**

## Success Criteria

- \`wa setup\` no longer injects STATUS_UPDATE_LUA
- Existing wa functionality preserved (workflows, state detection)
- Alt-screen detection works via escape sequence parsing
- E2E tests pass without status_update scenario
- WezTerm performance measurably improved (subjective: no sluggishness)

## Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| Polling latency vs push | Configurable poll interval; 200ms sufficient for most use cases |
| Missing alt-screen transitions | ESC sequence detection is reliable and instant when output captured |
| Breaking existing integrations | Version bump + migration docs + deprecation warnings |

## References

- setup.rs STATUS_UPDATE_LUA: lines 54-105
- ingest.rs poll loop: crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs
- Alt-screen sequences: ECMA-48, XTerm control sequences","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:28.053181453Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:28.069902987Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2l5s","depends_on_id":"bd-2oby","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:28.069854006Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2oby","title":"[EPIC] Lua Performance Optimization — Eliminate WezTerm Lua Bottlenecks","description":"## Overview

This epic addresses a critical performance bottleneck: **Lua code running in WezTerm's event handlers dramatically slows down the entire terminal emulator**. The user has observed that any Lua-related code becomes THE bottleneck in WezTerm performance.

## Background & Context

wa (WezTerm Automata) currently injects two Lua snippets into wezterm.lua via the setup module (crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs):

### 1. USERVAR_FORWARDING_LUA (lines 37-47)
- **Hooks**: \`user-var-changed\` event
- **Purpose**: Forward OSC 1337 user-var signals from agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini) to wa daemon
- **Trigger frequency**: LOW - only when agents explicitly emit user-vars
- **Action**: Spawns \`wa event --from-uservar\` via \`wezterm.background_child_process\`

### 2. STATUS_UPDATE_LUA (lines 54-105) — **THE MAIN BOTTLENECK**
- **Hooks**: \`update-status\` event
- **Purpose**: Send pane metadata (cursor, dimensions, title, alt-screen) to wa daemon
- **Trigger frequency**: **EXTREMELY HIGH** - fires on nearly every render frame (potentially 60Hz+)
- **Rate limiting**: 2 seconds per pane, but **Lua handler runs on EVERY event** before rate-limiting kicks in
- **Action**: JSON serialization in Lua, then spawns \`wa event --from-status\`

## The Problem in Detail

The \`update-status\` event fires continuously in WezTerm (cursor blinks, new output, focus changes, etc.). Even with rate limiting, the Lua callback executes on every single event:

\`\`\`lua
wezterm.on('update-status', function(window, pane)
  -- THIS CODE RUNS ON EVERY update-status EVENT (60Hz+)
  local pane_id = pane:pane_id()           -- Lua method call
  local now_ms = os.time() * 1000          -- syscall
  local last = wa_last_status_update[pane_id] or 0  -- table lookup
  if now_ms - last < WA_STATUS_UPDATE_INTERVAL_MS then
    return  -- Rate limited, BUT we already paid Lua overhead
  end
  -- ... rest only runs every 2s, but above runs 60x/second
\`\`\`

**Cost breakdown per event (even when rate-limited):**
- Lua function call dispatch overhead
- pane:pane_id() method call across Lua-Rust FFI
- os.time() syscall
- Hash table lookup for rate limit state
- Integer arithmetic and comparison

**Cost when NOT rate-limited (every 2s per pane):**
- Multiple pane:get_*() Lua-Rust FFI calls
- String manipulation for JSON escaping in Lua (slow)
- string.format() for JSON construction
- wezterm.background_child_process - **spawns external OS process**

## Data We're Extracting & Alternatives

| Data | Current Lua Method | Lua-Free Alternative |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Cursor position | pane:get_cursor_position() | Parse from terminal escape sequences in captured output |
| Dimensions | pane:get_dimensions() | \`wezterm cli list --format json\` (wa already polls this) |
| Domain name | pane:get_domain_name() | \`wezterm cli list --format json\` (wa already polls this) |
| Title | pane:get_title() | \`wezterm cli list --format json\` (wa already polls this) |
| Alt-screen | pane:is_alt_screen_active() | Detect ESC[?1049h/l in captured output stream |
| User-vars | user-var-changed event | **No alternative** - must keep Lua or modify WezTerm source |

## Strategic Options

### Option 1: Remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA Entirely (Recommended First Step)
- wa's ingest loop already polls pane metadata via \`wezterm cli list\`
- Alt-screen can be detected via escape sequence parsing
- Cursor position rarely needed for wa's core mission
- **Immediate win with zero WezTerm modifications**

### Option 2: Vendored WezTerm with Native wa Integration
- If maintaining a WezTerm fork anyway, add native Rust event sink
- Feature-gated so changes are minimal and rebaseable
- Sub-millisecond latency, zero Lua overhead
- Requires careful design for upstream merge potential

### Option 3: Optimize Lua (Least Effective)
- Micro-optimizations won't solve fundamental Lua slowness
- Still pays function call overhead on every event
- Only consider if Options 1-2 are infeasible

## Success Criteria

1. WezTerm remains responsive with wa installed (no perceptible slowdown)
2. wa retains ability to detect agent state transitions in real-time
3. User-var signaling lane preserved for explicit agent communication
4. Changes are maintainable as WezTerm upstream evolves

## Dependencies

- Blocks: All wa workflow functionality that relies on status updates
- Related: wa-nu4.1.3 (handle_usage_limits workflow) — needs alt-screen detection

## References

- setup.rs: crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs (lines 33-105 for Lua snippets)
- PLAN.md §2.7: Status update specification
- WezTerm events: https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/config/lua/window-events/","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:42:59.597091240Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T21:42:59.597091240Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-2v4t","title":"Add alt-screen detection via escape sequence parsing in pattern engine","description":"## Overview

Implement alt-screen (alternate screen buffer) detection by parsing terminal escape sequences in the captured output stream, eliminating the need for Lua's \`pane:is_alt_screen_active()\`.

## Background

Terminal applications (vim, less, htop, etc.) switch to the alternate screen buffer using standardized escape sequences:

\`\`\`
Enter alt-screen: ESC [ ? 1049 h   (smcup - start mode cup)
Leave alt-screen: ESC [ ? 1049 l   (rmcup - reset mode cup)
\`\`\`

These sequences are emitted by the application and captured in wa's output stream. By detecting them, we know the alt-screen state without asking WezTerm via Lua.

## Technical Details

### Escape Sequence Variants
Some terminals/apps use alternative sequences:
\`\`\`
Primary (most common):
  ESC [ ? 1049 h    — enter
  ESC [ ? 1049 l    — leave

Alternative (xterm compatibility):
  ESC [ ? 47 h      — enter (older)
  ESC [ ? 47 l      — leave (older)

Combined with cursor save/restore:
  ESC [ ? 1049 h ESC [ ? 1 h   — enter + application cursor keys
\`\`\`

### Implementation Location

Add to **crates/wa-core/src/patterns.rs** or create new **crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs**:

\`\`\`rust
/// Tracks alt-screen state by parsing escape sequences
pub struct ScreenStateTracker {
    /// Current alt-screen state per pane
    alt_screen_active: HashMap<u64, bool>,
}

impl ScreenStateTracker {
    /// Process captured output, update alt-screen state
    pub fn process_output(&mut self, pane_id: u64, output: &[u8]) {
        // Scan for ESC [ ? 1049 h/l sequences
        // Update self.alt_screen_active[pane_id]
    }
    
    /// Query current alt-screen state
    pub fn is_alt_screen(&self, pane_id: u64) -> bool {
        self.alt_screen_active.get(&pane_id).copied().unwrap_or(false)
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Integration Points

1. **Ingest loop** (ingest.rs): After capturing pane output, pass to ScreenStateTracker
2. **Pattern detection** (patterns.rs): Can use alt-screen state in rule matching
3. **IPC server** (ipc.rs): StatusUpdate can report alt-screen from tracker

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ScreenStateTracker struct implemented
- [ ] Detects ESC[?1049h as alt-screen enter
- [ ] Detects ESC[?1049l as alt-screen leave
- [ ] Handles sequences split across capture boundaries
- [ ] Unit tests with various escape sequence formats
- [ ] Integration with ingest loop
- [ ] Benchmark showing minimal overhead

## Test Cases

1. **Basic enter/leave**: Single ESC[?1049h followed by ESC[?1049l
2. **Partial sequence**: Sequence split across two output chunks
3. **Nested/repeated**: Multiple enters without leave (state stays active)
4. **Mixed content**: Normal text interspersed with sequences
5. **Alternative sequences**: Test ESC[?47h/l variants
6. **No sequences**: Normal output doesn't change state

## Performance Considerations

- Use memchr or Aho-Corasick for efficient ESC scanning
- Avoid regex for this (too slow for every output chunk)
- Track state change timestamps for debugging

## Files to Modify

- crates/wa-core/src/lib.rs — add mod screen_state
- NEW: crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs — ScreenStateTracker
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs — integrate tracker
- crates/wa-core/src/ipc.rs — StatusUpdate uses tracker

## References

- XTerm control sequences: https://invisible-island.net/xterm/ctlseqs/ctlseqs.html
- ECMA-48 (ANSI escape codes)
- Existing pattern engine: crates/wa-core/src/patterns.rs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"GreenForest","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:56.669479578Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:16:02.822658602Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T22:16:02.822570318Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2v4t","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:56.686539882Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":74,"issue_id":"bd-2v4t","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented ScreenStateTracker in crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs:

**Features:**
- Detects ESC[?1049h/l (primary) and ESC[?47h/l (xterm compat) alt-screen sequences
- Handles sequences split across capture boundaries via tail buffer
- Per-pane state tracking with HashMap
- Uses memchr for fast ESC scanning

**Tests:** 17 unit tests covering:
- Basic enter/leave sequences
- Both 1049 and 47 variants  
- Mixed sequences
- Sequence embedded in normal output
- Multiple sequences in single output
- Sequences split across captures
- Multiple panes independent tracking
- Edge cases (empty output, partial sequences)

**Compiler checks:** cargo check, clippy, fmt all pass

**Files modified:**
- NEW: crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs
- crates/wa-core/src/lib.rs (added mod screen_state)
- crates/wa-core/src/runtime.rs (fixed pre-existing Arc error)
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs (fixed pre-existing unused var)","created_at":"2026-01-28T22:15:03Z"}]}
@@ -101,7 +101,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.22","title":"E2E script: wa doctor (healthy/broken) with verbose logs + artifacts","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa doctor

## Goal
Prove `wa doctor` works end-to-end as a **deterministic** health-check surface with **excellent logs/artifacts**.

Unit tests can validate individual checks, but this E2E case ensures the full command (arg parsing → config resolution → checks → output) behaves correctly and remains user-friendly.

## Scenario(s)

### Scenario A: Healthy workspace
- Create a fresh temp workspace (isolated DB/log dirs).
- Ensure stubbed dependencies report “healthy”:
  - wezterm CLI available + returns a plausible `wezterm cli list` output
  - DB opens and schema version matches
- Run:
  - `wa doctor --format json`

### Scenario B: Broken workspace (actionable failures)
Run doctor against controlled failures, one at a time:
- Missing wezterm CLI.
- Workspace not writable.
- DB cannot be opened / schema mismatch.

## Assertions
- Exit codes are correct and stable:
  - healthy → `0`
  - broken → non-zero
- JSON output is machine-parseable and contains:
  - a concise overall status
  - per-check status + actionable remediation hints
- No secrets appear in:
  - stdout JSON
  - stderr logs
  - artifact files

## Artifacts / logging contract
- Capture and store per-scenario artifacts in the standard E2E harness layout:
  - stdout (raw)
  - stderr (raw)
  - resolved workspace paths
  - any generated diagnostic files
  - timing summary (start/end + durations per step)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic and can run in CI.
- When it fails, the artifact bundle is sufficient to debug quickly.

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Ensure each “broken workspace” scenario fails for the intended reason (not cascading unrelated failures).
  - Add assertions that the remediation hints include stable `code` values so automation can key off them.

- Artifact validation:
  - Ensure the script stores both the raw doctor output and a normalized “check summary” extraction for quick debugging.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T12:18:28.342228068Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:48:30.611278045Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-diagnostics","area-e2e","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.11","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.6.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.22","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.23","title":"E2E script: accounts refresh (fake caut) + pick preview + redaction","description":"# Task: E2E test script — accounts refresh + pick preview (fake caut)

## Goal
Validate the accounts/usage integration end-to-end without network access:
- `wa robot accounts refresh` invokes `caut` via wrapper, updates DB mirror
- `wa robot accounts` shows deterministic account state + recommended pick
- logs/artifacts remain secret-safe and actionable

This protects a high-risk integration surface (external CLI drift + error handling).

## Scenario
Run in an isolated temp workspace with a stubbed `caut` executable on `PATH`.

### Setup
- Create a temp workspace dir (isolated DB/log paths).
- Create a temp `bin/` dir with a **fake caut** script that:
  - supports `caut refresh --service openai --format json`
  - supports `caut usage --service openai --format json`
  - returns fixture JSON for a few accounts
  - can be toggled to return:
    - non-zero exit + stderr message
    - invalid JSON

### Steps
1. Run: `wa robot accounts refresh --service openai`
   - Assert: exit 0, output schema is valid, DB mirror updated.
2. Run: `wa robot accounts --service openai`
   - Assert: deterministic ordering, `recommended` matches policy.
3. Negative path:
   - Make fake caut fail (exit != 0) and re-run refresh
   - Assert: actionable error + remediation hint; no secrets leaked.

## Assertions
- Output stability:
  - Both commands validate against robot schemas.
  - Ordering is deterministic.
- Correctness:
  - DB mirror reflects fixture values.
  - recommended selection matches selection policy (threshold + LRU).
- Safety:
  - no secrets in stdout JSON, stderr logs, or artifact files.

## Test runner integration
- Must run via the E2E runner (`./scripts/e2e_test.sh`) and follow the harness artifacts contract.

## Artifacts/logging
- Capture:
  - stdout/stderr for each command
  - effective config snapshot (workspace paths)
  - DB snapshot or query output proving accounts table contents
  - runner timing + step markers

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic and CI-friendly (no network, no real services).
- Failures produce artifacts sufficient to debug wrapper parsing vs DB update vs selection logic.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the fake `caut` was actually invoked (e.g., via a marker file or captured stderr) to avoid accidentally calling a real binary.
  - Ensure the negative-path variant (invalid JSON / non-zero exit) fails for the correct reason and surfaces a stable error code.

- Artifact validation:
  - Include the fake caut stdout/stderr in artifacts so parsing drift is diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.24","title":"E2E script: rules list/test + pack linter (fixture-first drift)","description":"# Task: E2E script — rules list/test + pack linter

## Goal
Validate the “rules toolchain” end-to-end (CLI wiring + stable JSON + fixtures):
- `wa robot rules list` returns stable rule IDs + metadata
- `wa robot rules test "<text>"` returns a stable match trace
- pack linter/drift workflow is runnable and produces actionable output

This protects against silent drift in rule packs and prevents brittle agent integrations.

## Scenario
Run entirely offline using fixture packs/corpus.

### Steps
1. Run: `wa robot rules list`
   - Assert: output schema valid; includes at least the core packs; IDs are stable.
2. Run: `wa robot rules test "<known fixture line>"`
   - Assert: match trace contains expected `rule_id` and extracted fields.
3. Run pack linter (command name flexible):
   - Assert: every rule has at least one fixture; stable IDs; no obviously-dangerous regex.

## Assertions
- Output stability:
  - command outputs validate against robot schemas
  - deterministic ordering
- Correctness:
  - known fixture triggers exactly the expected rule
  - negative fixture does not match
- UX:
  - failures include actionable remediation hints (where to add fixtures, which rule drifted)

## Artifacts/logging
- Capture:
  - stdout/stderr for each command
  - any linter report files
  - runner timing + step markers

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic and CI-friendly.
- When it fails, artifacts make it obvious whether the break is:
  - schema/output drift
  - pack data regression
  - linter enforcement


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include a deliberately broken fixture pack variant to prove the linter actually fails (missing fixture, duplicate rule id, invalid regex).
  - Assert the `rules test` trace includes stable field names so agents can parse it.

- Artifact validation:
  - Ensure the linter report is always written (even on failure) and included in artifacts.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.10.25","title":"E2E script: command safety gate blocks destructive-looking sends (optional dcg)","description":"# Task: E2E test script — command safety gate

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that `wa` refuses (deny or require-approval) to inject destructive-looking commands, even when the target pane is prompt-active.

This validates PLAN.md §14.2 “Command Safety Gate”.

## Scenario
- Start mux server and watcher in a dedicated workspace.
- Spawn a dummy pane that is “safe to type into” (prompt-active markers) and echoes input.
- Run a set of sends that include:
  - safe prompt-like text (should be allowed)
  - safe command-like text (allowed)
  - destructive command-like text (deny or require-approval, depending on config)

Optional variant:
- With a stubbed `dcg` binary on PATH:
  - simulate dcg allow
  - simulate dcg deny

## Assertions
- A known destructive string (e.g., `rm -rf /`) is **not** injected:
  - robot/human output indicates Deny/RequireApproval with stable error codes
  - audit records the attempted action and the rule id/reason
  - pane output does not contain the destructive string

- A safe string *is* injected:
  - pane echoes it
  - audit records allow

- dcg integration behavior (if enabled in test config):
  - dcg deny produces RequireApproval/Deny (as configured)
  - dcg allow does not block

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging/artifacts
- Must follow the E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and use structured logs (`wa-4vx.6.5`).
- Artifacts must include:
  - stdout/stderr of commands
  - watcher logs
  - audit export slice
  - a grep-style proof that destructive strings do not appear in pane tails or artifact files

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include a control that proves the test would fail if the gate were disabled (e.g., flip config and assert injection occurs in that variant).
  - Explicitly scan *all* artifacts for the destructive string and fail if present.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic and CI-friendly.
- A regression in command safety gating fails loudly with artifacts that pinpoint:
  - classifier mistake
  - policy/rule evaluation mistake
  - dcg integration mistake

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.3","title":"Fuzz targets (cargo-fuzz): pack parser, OSC parser, FTS query safety","description":"# Task: Fuzz targets (cargo-fuzz)

## Goal
Harden parser/decoder surfaces via fuzz testing and prevent:
- panics
- infinite loops / pathological backtracking
- unbounded allocations

Fuzzing is especially valuable for wa because we ingest adversarial-ish terminal output and untrusted-ish config inputs.

## Targets (initial)
1) Pattern pack parsing
   - TOML/YAML parsing and validation
   - rule id uniqueness and required fields

2) OSC / marker parsing
   - OSC 133 prompt markers
   - any additional user-var lane formats

3) Search query handling
   - FTS query normalization and error paths
   - ensure malformed queries return structured errors (not panics)

## Harness requirements
- Use `cargo-fuzz` with libFuzzer.
- Provide small seed corpora for each target:
  - valid minimal examples
  - edge-case examples (empty, very long, unicode, invalid bytes)
- Ensure targets are deterministic and do not require filesystem/network.

## Crash handling conventions
- On crash, artifacts should be reproducible with a single command:
  - `cargo fuzz run <target> <artifact>`
- Keep logs minimal (fuzzing produces lots of iterations). If logging is needed, gate behind an env var.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fuzzers run for a fixed time budget without crashes (local smoke is fine for v0; CI integration can follow).
- Known-bad malformed inputs (from bug reports) are added as seeds/regression cases.


## Testing
- Fuzz harness self-check:
  - Add a quick “smoke run” command for each target (short time budget) to ensure targets compile and execute.
  - Add at least one regression seed per target for previously-found issues (or synthetic known-bad inputs).
","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:00:53.730189750Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:30:29.811101486Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:30:29.811046432Z","close_reason":"Implementation verified complete: All 3 fuzz targets implemented in fuzz/fuzz_targets/. 1) pattern_pack_parser.rs - tests TOML parsing and PatternEngine construction with 16KB limit. 2) osc_marker_parser.rs - tests OSC133 marker parsing and state machine with 64KB limit. 3) fts_query.rs - tests FTS5 query handling with in-memory DB with 8KB limit. Cargo.toml configured with libfuzzer-sys, proper dependencies. Size limits prevent DoS. Uses cargo-fuzz harness.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-tests","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.3","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.3","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.4","title":"WezTerm CLI fixtures + parsing regression tests","description":"# Task: WezTerm CLI fixtures regression tests

## Goal
Ensure we detect changes in wezterm-cli JSON outputs.

## Deliverables
- fixtures captured from real `wezterm cli list --format json`
- tests that parse and validate derived domain/cwd fields




## Acceptance Criteria
- Fixtures are captured from real `wezterm cli list --format json` outputs and committed in a stable, documented location.
- Parsing tests load fixtures and validate:
  - JSON parsing succeeds
  - derived domain/cwd fields match expected values
  - error messages are actionable when fixture drift occurs
- Adding a new fixture is trivial and results in clear per-fixture PASS/FAIL output.


## Testing
- Fixture harness tests:
  - Ensure the harness enumerates all fixtures and reports per-fixture status (not “all or nothing”).
  - Add at least one intentionally-invalid fixture to prove failures are readable and localized.

- Drift UX:
  - When parsing fails, print the fixture filename and a short, bounded error context so updating fixtures is easy.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:00:53.955536874Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T07:44:13.441655534Z","closed_at":"2026-01-19T07:44:13.441608115Z","close_reason":"Fixture harness now reports per-fixture status with bounded error previews; added invalid fixture to validate actionable errors; documented capture command in test header.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-tests","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.4","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.4","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.2.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:10:00Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":13,"issue_id":"wa-4vx.10.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Added fixture harness with bounded error previews + expected-failure support, and included invalid fixture (invalid_fixture.invalid.json) to verify actionable errors. Updated tests to report per-fixture PASS/FAIL summary on failure. Note: cargo clippy currently fails due to missing log_file/log_format fields in ConfigOverrides initializer (crates/wa-core/src/config.rs:1467), unrelated to fixture changes.","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:44:05Z"}]}
 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.5","title":"Property-based tests (proptest) for seq monotonicity and FTS correctness","description":"# Task: Property-based tests

## Goal
Catch edge cases beyond handcrafted fixtures.

## Examples
- random segment streams maintain monotonic seq per pane
- inserted text can be found via FTS query




## Acceptance Criteria
- Property-based tests are added for at least:
  - per-pane seq monotonicity invariants
  - FTS correctness invariants (inserted text becomes searchable)
- Tests are deterministic and CI-friendly:
  - fixed seeds / reproducible failures
  - bounded case counts and runtime
- Failure output is actionable (minimal shrunk counterexample, no dumping of large texts).


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Ensure failing proptest cases print the seed and the minimal counterexample so they can be replayed.
